title: HIV prevention pill could cost less than $5 per person per year to make
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 15 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we hear of new drugs for the ‘undruggable’ KRAS protein, learn how RNA nanoparticle technology could revolutionise chronic disease therapy and find out that a bone marrow transplant from a sibling functionally cured a man of HIV.
Globally, as of 2024, there were 40.8 million people living with HIV. (NIH/Stocktrek Images/Getty)

HIV PReP pill costs less than US$5 to make

The MK 8527 pill — which is currently in late-stage clinical trials — could cost less than US$5 per person per year to mass produce, according to an analysis by medical researchers. The preventative pill takes effect within 24 hours and only has to be taken once a month, offering a more accessible alternative to injections or daily pills. Advocates are calling on the drug’s developer, the US pharma company Merck, to allow a low-cost generic version of the drug to be sold in poorer countries. “To shut down HIV transmission, we need to give preventive drugs to tens of millions of people,” says pharmacologist Andrew Hill. “These drugs need to be extremely cheap to make mass prevention affordable.”

STAT | 5 min read
 Reference: AIDS paper

Drugs for the ‘undruggable’ KRAS protein

Fresh approaches to drug design are bringing researchers closer to effective cancer treatments that target KRAS — a protein that, when mutated, fuels some of the most lethal tumours. Mutant KRAS was once considered to be ‘undruggable’, but a new type of drug that tags the protein for destruction by the body’s ‘waste disposal’ system has shown signs of success in a clinical trial. Another four trials are also exploring a separate drug that inhibits several different mutant forms of KRAS. In combinations, such treatments could create a regimen that KRAS-mutant cancers cannot escape, says gastroenterologist Dieter Saur.

Nature | 5 min read

One CAR-T to cure them all

A woman with an ultra-rare combination of three autoimmune diseases has had no symptoms since receiving a single dose of engineered immune cells. Researchers used the woman’s own T cells to develop chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells that targeted a protein on her B cells — the culprits behind her trifecta of autoimmune conditions. The woman experienced no side effects from the treatment, and fourteen months on she doesn’t take any medication for her conditions, says haematologist Fabian Müller, who helped to develop the treatment.

Nature | 5 min read
Read more about early data from a phase II trial of off-the-shelf CAR-T cells in EndPoints News.
 Reference: Med paper

Sibling bone marrow transplant cures HIV

A man has been functionally cured of HIV after receiving a bone marrow transplant from his sibling. The researchers found no detectable HIV in the recipient’s blood, gut tissue or bone marrow after the transplant. “I think we have shown, for the first time, that it’s a complete engraftment — both in peripheral blood, which has been shown in several other cases, in bone marrow, which has also been shown in a couple of other cases, and then also the gut mucosal tissue, which we think is key for [a] cure,” says infectious disease specialist and study co-author Marius Trøseid. A handful of people have been functionally cured of HIV using bone marrow transplants before, but the use of a sibling donor helps avoid some of the complications of a transplant.

Scientific American | 6 min read
 Reference: Nature Microbiology paper

Infographic of the week
RNA nanoparticle technology could revolutionize chronic disease therapy and the treatment of conditions that cause permanent damage to major organs by helping to better target drugs to organs, write a group of 14 medical researchers. But there are still barriers to overcome in terms of clinical translation, such as the antiviral response triggered by foreign RNA. (Communications Medicine | 42 min read) (Bakrania, A., Nguyen Lefebvre, AT., de Vries, R. et al./Commun. Med.)

Approvals of the month

  • Tividenofusp alfa (Avlayah) gains approval for rare metabolic disease Hunter Syndrome: The drug is the first FDA-approved drug designed from living cells that crosses the blood brain barrier. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 4 min read)
  • Psoriasis drug nabs FDA approval: Icotrokinra (Icotyde) is a once-daily pill targeting the receptor for interleukin-23 — a protein involved in inflammation. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 4 min read)
  • FDA approves gene therapy for ultra-rare disease severe leukocyte adhesion deficiency type 1: The therapy is expected to cost millions of dollars. (STAT | 5 min read)
  • US approval for the second GLP-1 pill for weight loss: The approval of the orfoglipron pill marks the fastest approval in a decade. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 4 min read)

Features and opinion

How to achieve Alzheimer’s prevention

A group of international experts have released a framework for evaluating drugs designed to stop or slow the onset of Alzheimer’s disease. Currently, there are no approved therapies for people who are at risk or have elevated biomarkers but who are not yet symptomatic, although clinical trials are underway. The framework outlines who preventative treatments should be designed for, on the basis of factors such as biomarker levels, safety expectations and benchmarks for effectiveness. The group adds that public-private partnerships and risk-sharing will be essential to ensure treatments are kept affordable and accessible. Building the evidence around biomarkers will also be key to regulatory approval and public acceptance, they note.

Nature Medicine | 37 min read
Read more about innovative breakthroughs in the Alzheimer’s disease drug industry in this perspective in npj drug discovery.

Quote of the week

“Understanding is still our human domain. And we need to understand not only the physics of small-molecule interactions, but the workings of our own tools.”

Medicinal chemist Derek Lowe argues that AI models for evaluating drug affinity, such as the open-source Boltz-2 and Alphafold 3, aren’t there yet when it comes to binding predictions for drugs and their targets. (Science | 8 min read)

Some actions can leave a permanent mark: the Royal Astronomical Society (RAS) fears that SpaceX and Reflect Orbital's plans to launch one million satellites and use space mirrors to reflect sunlight to Earth, respectively, could be two such actions. The RAS, European Southern Observatory and International Astronomical Union have all submitted oppositions to the plans, over fears that both actions could leave permanent scars on the night sky. There are also concerns over health implications.

Please send your favourite constellation — and your feedback — to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Jacob Smith & Flora Graham

summary.overview: 이번 호는 HIV 예방 약물, 암 치료용 신약, CAR-T 치료, HIV 치유 기술, 알츠하이머 예방 전략 등 다양한 생명공학 및 의학 분야의 최신 연구를 다루고 있습니다. 각 기사에서 제시된 혁신적인 접근법과 임상 결과를 요약합니다.
covered_item_titles: HIV PReP 약물 제조 비용이 5달러 미만으로 낮아질 수 있음
‘불가능한’ KRAS 단백질에 대한 신약 개발 전망
단일 CAR-T 치료로 세 가지 자가면역 질환 완치 사례
형제 뼈髓 이식으로 HIV 치유 성공 사례
알츠하이머 예방을 위한 국제적 평가 프레임워크 제시

article_briefings.title: HIV PReP 약물 제조 비용이 5달러 미만으로 낮아질 수 있음
article_briefings.briefing_ko: MK 8527이라는 HIV 예방 약물은 대량 생산 시 1인당 연간 5달러 미만으로 제조할 수 있을 것으로 분석됐으며, 24시간 이내 효과가 나타나며 간단한 복용이 가능합니다.

article_briefings.title: ‘불가능한’ KRAS 단백질에 대한 신약 개발 전망
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 전통적인 약물 설계 기법을 벗어난 새로운 접근법으로 KRAS 돌연변이를 표적으로 하는 암 치료제 개발이 가속화되고 있으며, 이는 생명-threatening한 종양 치료에 희망을 제공하고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: 단일 CAR-T 치료로 세 가지 자가면역 질환 완치 사례
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 환자의 T세포를 활용한 CAR-T 치료로 극소수의 세 가지 자가면역 질환을 동시에 치료해 증상이 사라졌으며, 이는 맞춤형 면역 치료의 가능성을 보여줍니다.

article_briefings.title: 형제 뼈髓 이식으로 HIV 치유 성공 사례
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 형제의 뼈髓 이식을 통해 HIV 감염자가 혈액, 소장 조직, 뼈髓에서 바이러스 검출이 없어졌으며, 이는 HIV 치료에 대한 새로운 전략으로 주목받고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: 알츠하이머 예방을 위한 국제적 평가 프레임워크 제시
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 국제 전문가들이 알츠하이머 예방 약물의 효과를 평가하는 기준을 제시하며, 현재는 위험 인자 보유자에 대한 치료법이 없어 이 프레임워크가 중요성이 높아졌습니다.

raw_metadata.subject: HIV prevention pill could cost less than $5 per person per year to make
